quiz.name: Session 09 Final Practice Quiz: SOC Workflow and Automation
quiz.intro: 5 short-answer and 2 long-answer questions assessing practical session competencies. Compare your answers with the sample answers in the .md version.

questiontext.text:
You receive the following alert:

Rule: OUTBOUND_CLEARTEXT_CREDENTIALS
Host: hr-ws-011, User: t.brown (HR)
Details: HTTP POST to http://192.168.50.100/login with credentials in plaintext
Destination: Internal IP in Engineering network segment
Time: 10:15 UTC (business hours)

Classify this alert (TP / FP / Benign TP) and explain your reasoning. What additional information would change your classification?

graderinfo.text: Strong answer identifies: ambiguous (Benign TP or TP), explains why detection is technically correct, lists specific additional context needed (CMDB lookup for 192.168.50.100, user's normal access patterns, whether this is a known internal app). Full marks for correct classification reasoning and at least 2 specific additional info items.

questiontext.text:
Your queue at 09:00 contains:

CaseSeverityCreatedStatus
AP108:45NEW
BP207:30In Progress
CP301:00Pending
DP208:55NEW

List these in order of priority and explain why. Identify any SLA risks.

graderinfo.text: Correct order: A → D → B → C. Case A P1 acknowledge SLA may already be breached at 09:00. Case D acknowledge SLA expires at 09:25. Case B resolve SLA at 11:30. Case C Pending — SLA clock may be paused. Full marks for correct order with SLA deadline calculations and risk identification.

questiontext.text:
Write an SBAR escalation brief for this scenario:

At 14:30 UTC, an alert fired for "Scheduled task created with encoded PowerShell command" on finance-ws-042. The task runs as m.chen (Finance). You decoded the base64 command and found it downloads a file from pastebin.com/raw/abc123 and executes it. The host has access to the financial reporting system.

graderinfo.text: All four SBAR components required: S (confirmed malicious task, what/when/where), B (specific technical details — base64 decoded, pastebin URL, financial system access), A (P1 assessment, ATT&CK technique if known), R (specific next actions — isolate, memory dump, check initial access). Full marks for all four components with specific technical detail.

questiontext.text:
A SOC team has a "Phishing Email" playbook that: (1) extracts sender IP, (2) queries VirusTotal, (3) if VT score > 5: creates TheHive case; (4) if VT score ≤ 5: closes as false positive.

Identify THREE weaknesses in this playbook design and explain how you would fix each.

graderinfo.text: Any 3 of: (1) Single enrichment source - new/unknown malicious IPs have low VT scores (fix: multi-source enrichment); (2) Auto-close as FP is dangerous (fix: require analyst confirmation); (3) No attachment or URL checking (fix: add hash and URL enrichment steps); (4) No domain age check (fix: add WHOIS/domain age); (5) No check for similar alerts from same sender (fix: SIEM correlation query). Full marks for 3 valid weaknesses each with a specific fix.

questiontext.text: Your SOC's MTTR for P3 incidents improved from 14 hours to 9 hours after implementing SOAR enrichment automation. However, a major data breach was not detected for 3 weeks. Which metric would have caught this gap? Explain.

graderinfo.text: Key insight: MTTD (detection coverage/dwell time) is the missing metric. MTTR improvement is irrelevant if the attack was never detected. The gap could be a detection coverage gap (ATT&CK technique not covered) or a false positive dismissal issue. Full marks for identifying MTTD + explaining the distinction between detection speed and detection coverage.

questiontext.text:
Design a complete SOAR playbook for a "Brute Force — RDP Gateway" alert (triggers on 50+ failed RDP attempts from single external IP in 15 min).

  1. (5 pts) Define inputs, trigger, and outputs.
  2. (10 pts) Write all steps in YAML pseudocode with decision gates.
  3. (5 pts) Identify which steps require human approval and justify.
  4. (5 pts) Describe how you would test this safely.

graderinfo.text:
Marking (25 pts):

(a) 5 pts: Inputs include source_ip, failed_count, any_successful_logins. Trigger defined. Outputs include risk_score, case_id, action_taken.

(b) 10 pts: Parallel enrichment (VT + AbuseIPDB + geo), risk scoring, P1 branch on successful login, severity branch on score, notification. YAML structure correct.

(c) 5 pts: Firewall block requires human approval (blast radius / false positive risk). Justified with specific reason. Account lockout is optional human gate.

(d) 5 pts: Mock environment, known-malicious test IPs, test both success and failure paths, non-production firewall for block tests, never test with real block in production.

questiontext.text:
A healthcare SOC: 4 analysts, 24/7, 12,000 alerts/month, 92% FP rate, MTTD 6h, P2 SLA met only 65%. Night shift analyst spends 4/8 hours on FP processing. No SOAR.

  1. (5 pts) Identify root causes of P2 SLA failure. What data would confirm your hypotheses?
  2. (10 pts) Design a 90-day improvement plan with specific, measurable actions.
  3. (5 pts) If you could implement only ONE change in week 1, what would it be and why?
  4. (5 pts) Write 3 success metrics to evaluate your plan at 90 days.

graderinfo.text:
Marking (25 pts):

(a) 5 pts: Hypotheses include night shift overload (single analyst + 50% FP time), manual enrichment bottleneck, possible shift transition gaps. Data: P2 breach time distribution, per-analyst breach rate, enrichment time logs.

(b) 10 pts: Three phases across 30/60/90 days, each with specific actions. Include FP reduction, SOAR deployment, and process changes. Each action has a measurable target.

(c) 5 pts: Best answer is FP rule suppression (highest leverage, immediate impact, no tools needed). Accept SOAR deployment if well-justified, but note the 3-6 month timeline.

(d) 5 pts: Three SMART metrics (specific, measurable). Examples: P2 SLA compliance % target, FP rate target, night shift FP processing time target.
